paragraphs.body: “Concrete Jungle, where dreams wither like flowers in drought. A labyrinth of concrete and steel, a battleground for souls lost and seeking.” (Ramirez)

Every morning, I was awoken to the scraping and sloshing of the scrubs and mops. My mom’s banging near my door to “levantate, floja!” “Get up lazy.” Though, I would always test my limit to about ten minutes until I’d hear her stomp closer. Whatever was in store for the weekend had already begun on a tiresome foot as I’d often help clean the kitchen because apparently vacuuming wasn’t enough the night before. My dad would come in, kissing me on my forehead, reminding me of my soccer game for later that day as was every other Saturday. He was my soccer team’s coach, much to my dismay, but I had gotten used to it. It was routine for us to go to the field an hour and a half before our set time for our games, so we had time to relax and practice skills with drills. Just as it was a routine for my team to cohesively warm up together, so was my dad’s constant pep talk to me. His final words always ending with “recuerda Becky, plata o plomo” “remember Becky, money or lead.” I would simply nod in a way to show I understood, but I never knew how far off I was.

annotations.text: His final words always ending with “recuerda Becky, plata o plomo” “remember Becky, money or lead.” I would simply nod in a way to show I understood, but I never knew how far off I was.
Every morning, I was awoken to the scraping and sloshing of the scrubs and mops. My mom’s banging near my door to “levantate, floja!” “Get up lazy.”

paragraphs.body: This completely random phrase was normally tossed around at family parties, carne asadas if I may specify, by my aunts and uncles. Though I knew Spanish and could speak it well, I never truly even cared to find what the meaning was. While my dad was mindlessly scrolling through telenovelas on the screen, I had asked him what it meant. He said it was a phrase he often heard back in the Lima, Peru. He told me as young as 13 years old, this phrase was what allowed him to survive. I didn’t know what he was talking about. “Plata O Plomo” is a phrase used notoriously by the Narcos, who meant it in a way of either you take the money, or you take a bullet and lose your life. My mouth was ajar. Where did he even learn this? Why did most of my family know this at all? Sighing, my dad told me of a past he was pained, almost ashamed of, full of vile, almost unrealistic stories I have only heard in the plotlines of a movie. My dad recounted his participation, along with his brother and cousin, of planting and harvesting cocoa leaves for a known gang prominent in the city of Peru. After harvesting, a few of these gang members would exchange money, which my dad desperately needed to pay for school and buy shoes as he only wore chanclas his whole life. He would continue to do this as a side hustle until he married my mom.

annotations.comment: I thought this was a kind of meat! Is it also a party? Which is all to say, the way you introduce your own language and details into this essay is brilliant.

paragraphs.body: “Blood stains the pavement, a tragic tapestry of lives cut short. Innocence and hope, casualties of hatred, a cycle of violence that knows no end.” (Ramirez)

annotations.comment: This is a fascinating way to structure this essay--the repetition of the poem, the structural motif it creates, is great.

paragraphs.body: Along the downtown areas of Peru where my family was raised in, crime was off the charts. Normal civilians would be tossed into the gang's affairs unknowingly and unaware that every second their lives were at stake. It’s as if “plata o plomo” embodied the mindset of those who lived in Lima, specifically. It surely embodied the mindset of my father. It was his form of survival that brought him to America. While from my point of view, my father was quite resilient and admirable for this, he never saw it as that. He felt I would be ashamed of his past and would be weirded out in a way of how he grew up providing the source of cocaine to these ruthless men. Though I was initially alarmed, I knew my dad. I knew how much he loved our family. I knew how much he was willing to sacrifice for us. Though if I didn’t know my dad, I would question everything, even his morality. It was then I realized how similar we were.
“Mothers weep, as their sons and daughters are swallowed whole. Empty arms cradle broken hearts, as dreams turn to ash in the darkness.” (Ramirez)

Back in middle school, the area I lived in was diverse, but in the AAP program, there were maybe 2 other Hispanic kids I knew. It never truly phased me until we took civics in 8th grade. My teacher wanted our class to understand politics in a more interactive way by having us debate using standpoints from the different parties at the time. This was during the 2020 Democratic Presidential debates that were shown live on the news. My teacher split everyone into groups, and for some reason, everyone in my group decided to immediately assign me to immigration policies. I didn’t question it, but it did earn a scoff from my end. I could only speak from my family's experience of making their way to America, which was quite different from the assumption other classmates thought they would've come here. The biggest argument against my point was that most of those from Central and South America were gang members and would only raise the crime in America. Most of my classmates seem to nod to that statement, unaware of the battles my own family had dealt with these “gangs.” One guy in my own group even joked about Pablo Escobar probably being my uncle.

annotations.text: One guy in my own group even joked about Pablo Escobar probably being my uncle.
annotations.comment: again. Wow. I can't believe they said this to you.

annotations.comment: wow. what a detail...and a telling moment.

paragraphs.body: It was apparently an inside joke that no one should mess with me or one of my uncles would hunt them down. This was only fed by people in power who allowed these very assumptions to erupt into the media. These other classmates didn’t know me. They weren’t aware of how much my parents were willing to take the plomo and come to America than keep all the plata and stay in Lima to raise a family. All my family in America had come here to escape that very stereotype that the media and most people I knew generalized about us. Our tamales are the same as your omelets, besides the lack of cheese. Our leche Abuelita is the same as your hot chocolate, but less sweet. In a way we’re all so similar, but a mere assumption of a small percentage of individuals to base on a whole continent of lives will only separate us further.
“But in the shadows, a flicker of resilience ignites. A poet's pen becomes a weapon, and words become a sanctuary.” (Ramirez)
Citations: Ramirez, S. (2024, February 21). Exploring the harsh reality: Poems about gang violence. PoemVerse. https://poemverse.org
